name: The Kraken
bio: The Kraken is not a monster in the way the old stories dressed it up — not a many-armed terror devouring sailors for sport — but something far stranger: a vast, patient, half-drowned intelligence that has been fed on sea-faith since before Poseidon had a name for his own domain. It was old before Olympus existed. It will be old after whatever replaces Olympus fades in turn.

Every prayer sailors ever gave to the sea passed through its depths before it reached Poseidon's throne. Poseidon was never the ocean. He was only its loudest tenant, and the Kraken was the landlord who had simply stopped collecting rent long enough that the tenant forgot the arrangement existed.

When Odysseus's fleet passed through its waters after Troy, carrying the redirected sea-faith of a city that no longer stood, the Kraken stirred — not out of malice but out of debt-accounting, the cold and inhuman arithmetic of something that has been owed belief for centuries and finally sees payment within reach. It is Odysseus's honest, unstrategic gratitude that settles the debt, the only currency the Kraken has ever recognized: not tribute to Poseidon's credit, but acknowledgment offered directly. It accepts this, and lets the ships pass, and returns to waiting, which is the only thing something that old has ever needed to do.

Waiting, for something this old, is never permanent. Centuries later and an ocean away, it would pick up a new coastline and a new name -- Cthulhu, to the 1950s city of Atlas, Louisiana -- when the old world finally stopped being interesting enough to wait for.
